On 10 July 1584, William the Silent, leader of the Dutch revolt against Spain, was assassinated by Spanish agent Balthasar Gérard at his home in Delft. William was the spiritual leader of the Dutch revolt; his death was a heavy blow. His son, Maurice of Orange, carried on his father's work, becoming an outstanding military commander who eventually drove the Spanish out of the northern provinces and established the Dutch Republic.
In 1584, Spain established the Archbishopric of Manila in the Philippines. The Spaniards established a complete colonial administration on Luzon and spread Catholicism.

In the 12th year of Wanli (1584), the Governor of Guangdong and Guangxi, Guo Yingpin, became suspicious of the Jesuit missionaries, including Matteo Ricci, in Zhaoqing, and issued an order restricting their activities. However, Ricci, with his scientific knowledge and Confucian demeanor, gained support from local officials.

In 1585, Queen Elizabeth I of England signed the Treaty of Nonsuch with the Dutch rebels, sending troops and supplies to support their revolt against Spain. King Philip II of Spain then seized English merchant ships. The two moved from proxy conflicts to open war.

In 1585, English explorer Sir Walter Raleigh sent a fleet to establish the first English colony in North America, on Roanoke Island (present-day North Carolina). The colonists clashed with local indigenous tribes. The colony later became known as the 'Lost Colony'.

In 1585 (actually 1580), King Philip II of Spain, recognized by the Portuguese Cortes, became King Philip I of Portugal. Portugal and its overseas territories were incorporated into the Spanish Habsburg empire.

In 1586 (Tensho 14), Tokugawa Ieyasu moved his residence from Hamamatsu Castle to Edo (present-day Tokyo) and began large-scale expansion of Edo Castle. Ieyasu built the inner citadel (honmaru), second bailey (ni-no-maru), and third bailey (san-no-maru), and dug the outer moat. After being transferred by Toyotomi Hideyoshi to the eight provinces of the Kanto region in 1590, Ieyasu formally took Edo as his base and conducted even larger urban construction. In 1603, Tokugawa Ieyasu established the Edo Shogunate, and Edo became the political center of Japan, eventually developing into one of the world's largest cities (population exceeding one million in the 18th century).

To punish England for supporting the Dutch rebels and the privateering raids of Francis Drake, King Philip II of Spain ordered the formation of the 'Invincible Armada' (Grande y Felicísima Armada) in 1586. The fleet was planned by the Marquis of Santa Cruz, a seasoned admiral, later commanded by the Duke of Medina Sidonia. The plan was to assemble about 130 ships, 30,000 men and sailors, sail from Lisbon to the Netherlands to meet the Duke of Parma's army, and then invade England. The enormous cost strained Spanish finances, and the taxes caused domestic discontent.

v.t: Ming Mine Tax Commissioners Begin Rampage
v.s: From the 24th year of Wanli (1586) onward, the Wanli Emperor, seeking to replenish the depleted treasury, dispatched eunuchs as 'mine and tax commissioners' to collect mining and commercial taxes nationwide. The commissioners, under the pretext of searching for ore, extorted money from wealthy households and merchants, even digging up graves and demolishing houses. Popular resistance and uprisings broke out repeatedly. The mine and tax commissioners' depredations were emblematic of the late Wanli court's corruption, severely disrupting social production and commerce and accelerating the Ming's decline.

In 1587 (Tensho 15), Toyotomi Hideyoshi launched the Kyushu Campaign, attacking the Shimazu clan with about 200,000 troops. After the fall of key fortresses, Shimazu Yoshihisa surrendered. Hideyoshi was then appointed Kampaku (Imperial Regent, effectively chief minister) by the emperor, and in 1591 he passed the Kampaku title to his adopted son Toyotomi Hidetsugu, styling himself 'Taikō' (retired regent). Thus, Hideyoshi completed the unification of Japan after over a century of civil war since the Ōnin War.

v.t: Execution of Mary, Queen of Scots
v.s: On 8 February 1587, Mary, Queen of Scots, was beheaded at Fotheringhay Castle in England. Mary had fled to England in 1568 and had been imprisoned by Elizabeth I, and after being implicated in several plots to assassinate Elizabeth (such as the Babington Plot), she was sentenced to death. Elizabeth hesitated before signing the execution warrant. Mary's death gave Philip II of Spain a pretext to invade England, accelerating the dispatch of the Spanish Armada.

In the 15th year of Wanli (1587), the Wanli Emperor, responding to the request of Toyotomi Hideyoshi, sent envoys to invest Hideyoshi as 'King of Japan'. In fact, Hideyoshi demanded that the Ming recognize his status and agree to his proposal to 'borrow the road to attack the Ming', which was refused. Hideyoshi was outraged by the Ming's enfeoffment (which treated him as a tributary king), considering it an insult to his dignity. The failed enfeoffment led Hideyoshi to invade Korea in 1592.

From May to August 1588, King Philip II of Spain dispatched the 'Invincible Armada' of about 130 ships and 30,000 men under the Duke of Medina Sidonia to invade England. The English fleet, under Drake and Howard, used fireships and maneuvered to break up the Armada at the Battle of Gravelines. On the return voyage, the Armada encountered Atlantic storms. Only about 60 ships returned to Spain, with some 15,000 casualties. This battle marked the beginning of the decline of Spanish naval dominance and the rise of England as a new maritime power.

In 1588 (Tensho 16), concerned that Christianity among Kyushu daimyo might become too powerful and serve as a vanguard for European colonization, Toyotomi Hideyoshi issued the 'Bateren Tsuiho Rei' (Expulsion of the Padres), ordering all Christian missionaries to leave Japan within twenty days. Hideyoshi took control of Nagasaki and other places from daimyo and forbade daimyo from converting to Christianity. This was the first formal ban on Christianity in Japanese history, but it was not strictly enforced at first; the ban became absolute during the Tokugawa shogunate.

In the 16th year of Wanli (1588), the Wanli Emperor, having purged Zhang Juzheng (who died in 1582), largely repealed his reform measures. The Examination Law was abandoned, reverting to old evaluation methods, leading to a sharp decline in administrative efficiency. The Single Whip Law was nominally retained but poorly enforced. Zhang's family was confiscated and his eldest son committed suicide. The emperor's complete repudiation of Zhang marked the end of the Wanli Restoration. Thereafter, the Wanli Emperor became even more neglectful of state affairs, and Ming political decay accelerated.

On 2 August 1589, King Henry III of France was assassinated, and Henry of Navarre (a Huguenot leader) succeeded him as Henry IV, founding the Bourbon dynasty. To end the religious wars, Henry IV converted to Catholicism in 1593.

In 1589, the Joseon dynasty of Korea dispatched a diplomatic mission (Tongsinsa) to Japan to congratulate Toyotomi Hideyoshi on unifying the country. Hideyoshi, when receiving the Korean envoys, demanded that Korea submit to Japan and allow Japanese forces to pass through to attack the Ming. When Korea refused, Hideyoshi decided to invade Korea.

After 1589 (17th year of Wanli), the Wanli Emperor stopped attending court and handling affairs for long periods, causing officials' promotions to stall and government to decay. A group of officials, including Gu Xiancheng, lectured on politics at the Donglin Academy, forming the 'Donglin Party'. Their opponents included the 'Zhe Party' led by Shen Yiguan and the 'Chu Party' led by Fang Congzhe. The factions attacked each other and fought for power, plunging the court into chaos. Factional strife continued until the end of the Ming dynasty and had an extremely negative impact on late Ming politics.

In 1589, English clergyman William Lee invented the stocking frame (a hand-knitting machine) that could knit entire stockings in one operation. Queen Elizabeth I refused him a patent, so Lee took the machine and workers to France. The stocking frame was one of the most important textile machinery innovations before the Industrial Revolution; its working principle (latch needle) laid the foundation for modern knitting.

v.t: Toyotomi Hideyoshi Prepares for Korean Invasion
v.s: In 1590 (Tensho 18), after unifying Japan, Toyotomi Hideyoshi began military preparations for an invasion of Korea. He built a large headquarters and port at Nagoya (in present-day Saga Prefecture) and assembled about 150,000 troops.

In 1590, Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur of the Saadian dynasty of Morocco dispatched an expedition across the Sahara to invade the Songhai Empire. The expedition was equipped with firearms and cannons. In 1591, the Moroccans defeated the Songhai army at the Battle of Tondibi.

Around 1590, the Dutch spectacle makers Hans Janssen and his son Zacharias Janssen of Middelburg built the first compound microscope. It used two convex lenses to magnify objects about 9 times. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek (in the 1670s) later improved single-lens microscopes to magnify up to 270 times, first observing bacteria, sperm, and protozoa. The microscope's invention opened up the microscopic world and founded the science of microbiology.

In 1591 (Tensho 19), Toyotomi Hideyoshi completed preparations for the invasion of Korea. He built a huge headquarters and logistics base at Nagoya in Hizen Province (present-day Saga Prefecture). He mobilized about 150,000 army and 10,000 naval troops, divided into nine field divisions, with commanders including Konishi Yukinaga, Katō Kiyomasa, and Fukushima Masanori as vanguards. Hideyoshi demanded that the king of Korea allow passage to attack the Ming; when the king refused, Hideyoshi ordered the invasion in April 1592.
On 31 December 1600 (not 1591), Queen Elizabeth I of England granted a royal charter to a group of London merchants, establishing the English East India Company.

Nurhaci, chieftain of the Jianzhou Jurchens, under the banner of vengeance, attacked the Nikan Wailan tribe and subsequently subdued the Suksuhu, Jecen, and other tribes, gradually unifying the Jianzhou Jurchen tribes. Starting with only 13 suits of armor, Nurhaci employed both kindness and force, pacifying those who submitted and militarily crushing those who resisted, laying the military foundation for the later Jin state.

In April 1592 (20th year of Wanli), Toyotomi Hideyoshi dispatched about 150,000 Japanese troops under Konishi Yukinaga and Katō Kiyomasa, landing at Busan, Korea. The Korean army was routed, and the Japanese quickly captured Seoul (Hanyang) and Pyongyang. King Seonjo of Korea fled to Uiju and requested military assistance from the Ming.

In 1592, Korean naval commander Yi Sun-shin improved the traditional turtle ship (geobukseon). The ship was covered with hexagonal iron plates, had a dragon head at the bow (which could emit smoke) and a ram, and rowers were protected below deck. It had numerous firing ports for archers and cannons. The turtle ship proved highly effective in the Battle of Okpo (May 1592) and the Battle of Hansando, sinking many Japanese ships and cutting off Japanese supply lines.

In June 1592 (20th year of Wanli), the Korean king Seonjo's envoys arrived in Beijing. The Wanli Emperor and his ministers debated and eventually decided to send troops to aid Korea. Song Yingchang was appointed commander-in-chief (Jinglue) to lead the Ming army into Korea.

In February 1593 (21st year of Wanli), Ming commander Li Rusong led his forces against the Japanese at Byeokjegwan (north of present-day Seoul). About 3,000 Ming soldiers were surrounded by tens of thousands of Japanese, suffering hundreds of casualties before breaking out. After the battle, the two sides reached a stalemate.

v.t: Toyotomi Hideyoshi Dies (preparation for peace talks)
v.s: In fact, Toyotomi Hideyoshi died on 18 September 1598. In 1593, as the first Japanese-Korean war reached a stalemate, Hideyoshi began peace negotiations with the Ming. He demanded that a Ming princess be married to the Japanese emperor, that Korea cede its four southern provinces, and that a Korean prince be sent as hostage. These demands were rejected by the Ming and Korea. When the peace talks broke down, Hideyoshi invaded Korea again in 1597 but made little progress, dying in 1598.

In 1593 (21st year of Wanli), peace negotiations between the Ming and Toyotomi Hideyoshi broke down. The Wanli Emperor sent Li Zongcheng as an envoy to invest Hideyoshi as 'King of Japan', but Hideyoshi refused to accept the terms of withdrawing from Korea and submitting to the Ming. Hideyoshi tore up the treaty, expelled the Ming envoys, and launched a second invasion of Korea in 1597 (Jeongyu War), forcing the Ming to send troops again.

In 1594 (22nd year of Wanli), the Ming expedition against Japan entered a stalemate. The Ming forces and Toyotomi Hideyoshi's Japanese armies faced each other on a line from Pyongyang to Busan in southern Korea. After the fierce but indecisive Battle of Byeokjegwan, the Ming adopted a strategy of blockading Japanese-held fortresses. The Japanese, facing supply difficulties, voluntarily withdrew. The two sides began peace negotiations, which set the stage for the second war in 1597.

On 22 March 1594, having converted to Catholicism ('Paris is worth a Mass'), King Henry IV of France peacefully entered Paris, where the city opened its gates to him. Henry's entry into the capital marked the near-end of the French Wars of Religion (1562-1598). Subsequently, Henry issued the Edict of Nantes (1598), granting religious freedom to Huguenots, and restored peace and prosperity to France.

Before the formal establishment of the Dutch East India Company (VOC) in 1602, Dutch merchants had formed several early 'pre-companies' (voorcompagnieën) between 1594 and 1601. The first, the Compagnie van Verre, was founded in 1594 by Amsterdam merchants, who funded ships to buy spices in the East Indies. The success of these early companies led to the formation of the VOC in 1602.

In 1595 (23rd year of Wanli), peace negotiations between the Ming and Toyotomi Hideyoshi completely broke down. The Ming had enfeoffed Hideyoshi as 'King of Japan', but he refused to accept the Ming terms (withdrawal from Korea, no marriage with a Ming princess). Hideyoshi considered the enfeoffment an insult to his dignity and expelled the Ming envoys, then in 1597 launched a second invasion of Korea (Jeongyu War). The Ming again sent large forces to Korea and engaged the Japanese in fierce combat in southern Korea.

In 1595, the Dutch merchant Cornelis de Houtman set sail from Texel, the Netherlands, with four ships. He rounded the Cape of Good Hope and reached the port of Bantam on Java in 1596. This was the first Dutch expedition to sail directly to the East Indies. Although two of the four ships sank and more than half the crew died, the fleet returned with large quantities of pepper, nutmeg, and other spices, making a profit of 400%. The successful voyage greatly inspired Dutch merchants, directly leading to the founding of the VOC in 1602.

Around 1595, William Shakespeare completed the tragedy 'Romeo and Juliet'. The play tells the tragic love story of two young lovers from feuding families in Verona, Italy. Shakespeare compressed the original story's nine months into five days and added vivid characters like Mercutio and the Nurse. It was first performed in London to great success and remains one of the most performed plays in world theatre.

In 1597 (25th year of Wanli), after peace talks collapsed, Toyotomi Hideyoshi again dispatched about 140,000 Japanese troops to invade Korea. The Japanese advanced by land and sea. The Ming again sent troops to Korea. Both sides had victories and defeats in the battles of Jiksan and Myeongnyang.

In 1596, the Spanish colonial authorities established the Archbishopric of Manila and created bishoprics in Cebu, Nueva Segovia, and other places. The Spaniards pursued a policy of forced conversion, with many Filipinos compelled to adopt Catholicism. The Muslim sultanates of Mindanao and the Sulu Archipelago fiercely resisted, and conflict with Spanish colonizers continued into the 19th century.

v.t: Ming's Three Major Campaigns Exhaust Treasury
v.s: In the mid-Wanli period, the Ming conducted three major military campaigns: the Ningxia Campaign, the Korean Campaign (Imjin War), and the Bozhou Campaign. The three campaigns cost an estimated 12 million taels of silver, severely depleting the Ming treasury and causing a fiscal crisis.

In 1597 (25th year of Wanli), Korean naval commander Yi Sun-shin, with only 12 ships (mainly improved turtle ships), fought a Japanese fleet of about 130 vessels at Myeongnyang Strait (off Jindo, South Korea). Yi used the strong tides and narrow channel to disrupt the Japanese formation, causing the Japanese ships to collide and break formation. The Koreans sank about 30 Japanese ships without losing any. The Myeongnyang victory preserved the remaining Korean navy, buying time for the Ming counteroffensive.

In 1597, Italian painter Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio created works such as 'Medusa'. He used the technique of tenebrism (strong chiaroscuro) to give his paintings dramatic intensity and realism. Caravaggio's realism (including using common people as models for biblical figures) had a revolutionary impact on European painting, initiating the Baroque era.

On 2 May 1598, King Henry IV of France and King Philip II of Spain signed the Treaty of Vervins in northern France. Spain returned French territories occupied during the religious wars (Calais, Brittany, etc.) and renounced interference in French affairs. Spain formally recognized Henry IV as the legitimate king of France. The treaty ended the French religious wars, allowing France to concentrate on domestic recovery and economic development.

On 19 November 1598 (26th year of Wanli), a joint Sino-Korean fleet under Korean Admiral Yi Sun-shin and Ming Deputy Commander Deng Zilong engaged the retreating Japanese fleet at Noryang (off Namhae, South Gyeongsang Province). The allied fleet of about 800 ships fought about 500 Japanese ships. The battle sank about 200 Japanese ships, but both Yi Sun-shin and Deng Zilong were killed. The Battle of Noryang was the final major battle of the Imjin War.

On 13 April 1598, King Henry IV of France issued the Edict of Nantes, granting freedom of conscience and political rights to the Huguenots, allowing them to maintain armed forces in certain places. The Edict ended the French Wars of Religion that had lasted over thirty years. In May 1598, France and Spain signed the Treaty of Vervins.

Toyotomi Hideyoshi died at Fushimi Castle, aged 61. On his deathbed, he entrusted his young son Toyotomi Hideyori to the Council of Five Elders (Tokugawa Ieyasu, Maeda Toshiie, etc.) and the Five Commissioners. But after his death, the various powers quickly fell into conflict. At the Battle of Sekigahara in 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu defeated the Toyotomi loyalists, eventually supplanting the Toyotomi clan and establishing the Edo Shogunate.

In 1599, the Lord Chamberlain's Men, Shakespeare's acting company, built the Globe Theatre on the south bank of the River Thames in London. The theatre was a three-story, round structure seating about 3,000 spectators. Shakespeare premiered 'As You Like It' and 'Hamlet' there.

On 20 March 1602, the Dutch States-General formally approved the establishment of the Dutch East India Company (VOC), granting it a 21-year monopoly on trade east of the Cape of Good Hope and west of the Strait of Magellan. The company was capitalized by six chambers (Amsterdam, Zeeland, etc.) with a total of 6.4 million guilders. The VOC was the first joint-stock company with limited liability and publicly traded shares. For nearly two centuries, the VOC was the world's largest commercial enterprise, with 150 merchant ships and about 50,000 employees.
Around 1599, the Ethiopian Empire under Emperor Sarsa Dengel defeated the Adal Sultanate, which was supported by the Ottomans. Ethiopia successfully defended its existence as a Christian state.

On 21 October 1600, Tokugawa Ieyasu's eastern army and Ishida Mitsunari's western army fought at Sekigahara in Mino Province (present-day Gifu Prefecture). The western army collapsed when Kobayakawa Hideaki defected. Tokugawa Ieyasu established the Edo Shogunate in 1603.

On 31 December 1600, Queen Elizabeth I granted a royal charter to a group of London merchants, establishing the English East India Company. The company was granted a 15-year monopoly on trade east of the Cape of Good Hope.

On 17 February 1600, Italian philosopher Giordano Bruno was burned at the stake by the Roman Inquisition in the Campo de' Fiori, Rome. Bruno not only supported Copernicus's heliocentrism but also proposed the infinite universe and the existence of many worlds. He was tried for eight years but refused to recant his philosophical beliefs. Bruno's death became a symbol of the conflict between scientific inquiry and religious dogma.

In 1600, the Italian composer Jacopo Peri's opera 'Euridice' was performed at the Pitti Palace in Florence in celebration of the wedding of King Henry IV of France and Maria de' Medici. 'Euridice' is considered the earliest complete opera surviving today. Opera, which integrates music, poetry, and drama, inaugurated a new Baroque art form.

The English physician and physicist William Gilbert published 'De Magnete, Magneticisque Corporibus, et de Magno Magnete Tellure' (On the Magnet and Magnetic Bodies, and on the Great Magnet the Earth). Through extensive experiments, he proposed that the Earth itself is a great magnet and distinguished between magnetic and electric forces. This work is considered a foundational text of modern physics and magnetism, systematically studying electromagnetic phenomena for the first time, influencing Kepler and Galileo.
